topic: A teacher receives an unexpected gift from a former student
raw_output:
[story 1]

Professor Eleanor Vance stared at the small, intricately carved wooden bird sitting on her desk. The presentation had been so sudden, so out of the blue, that it had completely derailed her afternoon. It was from Thomas, a student she had mentored through three challenging years. His usual demeanor, so earnest and focused in the lecture hall, was replaced by a hesitant awkwardness as he offered the gift. Eleanor felt a strange tightening in her chest, a sudden, unwelcome weight settling there. She picked up the bird, its polished surface cool against her fingertips. Her throat felt suddenly dry, and she found herself avoiding his gaze, focusing instead on the dust motes dancing in the afternoon sunlight slicing through the window. She offered a polite, strained smile, the kind one reserves for unavoidable social obligations. "Thomas," she managed, her voice a little too level, "this is… unexpected." The silence that followed seemed vast, filled only with the faint hum of the ventilation system.

[story 2]

I watched the package sit on my kitchen counter, the wrapping paper a garish shade of blue that felt entirely too cheerful for the mood I was currently inhabiting. It was from Sarah, a bright, eager student from my advanced literature class, whom I had guided through her early writing struggles. When she handed it over, her eyes were wide, brimming with an intensity that made my stomach clench uncomfortably. I took the box, my fingers brushing hers briefly, and immediately pulled back, as if the contact had sparked something unpleasant. I turned the object over in my hands, tracing the edges of the wrapping with my thumb. A dull ache bloomed behind my ribs, a sensation akin to a sudden drop in temperature. I mumbled a thank you, the words feeling clumsy and forced, and immediately turned to the kettle, desperate for the mundane rhythm of boiling water to drown out the sudden, sharp awareness of her presence and the weight of the gesture.

[story 3]

The small, velvet pouch felt heavy in my palm as I finally opened it. Inside was a collection of rare pressed flowers, a painstaking arrangement clearly made with great care. It was from David, a student whose passion for botany had been both inspiring and, lately, deeply disappointing. When he approached my office door, his usual easy confidence was replaced by a visible tremor in his hands. I looked up, and for a fleeting second, I saw a flicker of something—perhaps apology, perhaps just nervousness—in his eyes. That glance sent a ripple of unease through me. I took the flowers, my grip tightening instinctively until my knuckles showed white. I didn't meet his eyes as I spoke, my tone carefully neutral. "David," I said, my voice measured and cool, "I appreciate the thought, truly. But I don't feel I need anything." The air in the room seemed to thicken, making every breath feel labored.
